csf_subcategory_name: Assets are prioritized based on classification and criticality
question: Have you documented the systems
column9: or transmit ePHI (electronic Protected Health Information) and have those ePHI-processing assets been classified and prioritized?
column10: ePHI data flow diagram or data map, ePHI system inventory, asset classification records identifying ePHI-scoped systems
column11: HIPAA §164.308(a)(1)(i) Risk Analysis — prerequisite for scoping all ePHI-specific safeguard assessments; complete this question before responding to Q2–Q9

csf_subcategory_name: Physical access to assets is managed monitored and enforced commensurate with risk
question: Are physical safeguards in place for locations where ePHI is accessed or stored — including workstation use policies and workstation security measures — and if your workforce operates remotely or in a hybrid model, are equivalent physical security controls enforced for off-site environments?
evidence: On-site: facility access control documentation (badge readers, locks, visitor logs), workstation physical security (screen locks, cable locks, secure positioning); Remote/hybrid: workstation use policy, VPN enforcement records, screen lock configuration, clean desk and screen privacy policy for home environments
notes: HIPAA §164.310(a)(1) Facility Access Controls; §164.310(b) Workstation Use; §164.310(c) Workstation Security — remote/hybrid qualifier: for workforces with no fixed facility, evidence shifts from physical access logs to workstation use policy enforcement and screen privacy controls

csf_subcategory_name: Assets are formally managed throughout removal transfers and disposition
question: Do you have documented procedures for sanitizing or physically destroying devices and electronic media containing ePHI before disposal or re-use, and are disposal and re-use activities logged with records of what was disposed of and by whom?
evidence: Data and media disposal policy referencing approved sanitization methods (e.g. NIST SP 800-88), media sanitization or destruction records, certificate of destruction, asset retirement logs, ePHI removal verification records before media re-use
notes: HIPAA §164.310(d)(1)(i) Disposal (R); §164.310(d)(1)(ii) Media Re-use (R) — both required specifications; disposal records also support §164.310(d)(1)(iii) Accountability (A)

question: Does your organization provide HIPAA-specific security awareness training to all workforce members who access ePHI, and is this training documented and refreshed at least annually?
evidence: Training materials referencing HIPAA Security Rule obligations, completion records for all ePHI-accessing staff, onboarding training checklist, annual refresh documentation, periodic security reminders (policy acknowledgments, newsletters, or phishing simulations)
notes: HIPAA §164.308(a)(5) Security Awareness and Training — addressable specifications addressed: §164.308(a)(5)(i) Security Reminders, §164.308(a)(5)(ii) Protection from Malicious Software (awareness component), §164.308(a)(5)(iii) Log-in Monitoring (awareness component), §164.308(a)(5)(iv) Password Management (training component)

csf_subcategory_name: A cybersecurity supply chain risk management program is established
question: Have you executed a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with each Covered Entity you handle ePHI for, and do you have BAAs in place with any subcontractors or vendors who access ePHI on your behalf?
evidence: Executed BAA documents (existence confirmed — content sufficiency review is out of scope), list of Covered Entity clients with BAA status, subcontractor and vendor list with BAA status for those accessing ePHI
notes: HIPAA §164.308(b)(1) Business Associate Contracts (R); §164.314(a)(2)(i) Business Associate Subcontractor Agreements (R) — assessment is limited to BAA existence verification; legal review of BAA content or sufficiency is explicitly out of scope

csf_subcategory_name: Internal and external stakeholders are notified of incidents
question: Do you have documented procedures for notifying your Covered Entity client(s) of a breach or suspected breach of unsecured ePHI — including the required notification content elements, the 60-day notification deadline from discovery, and law enforcement delay provisions per §164.412?
evidence: Breach notification procedure document, incident response plan with breach escalation section, notification templates referencing §164.410 content requirements (nature of breach, PHI involved, unauthorized persons, mitigation steps, contact information), list of CE notification contacts, documented §164.412 law enforcement delay hold process
notes: HIPAA §164.410 Notification by Business Associates (R); §164.412 Law Enforcement Delay (R) — BA must notify CE without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days from discovery; notification must include specific content elements enumerated in §164.410(c)

csf_subcategory_name: The recovery portion of the incident response plan is executed
question: Do you have a contingency plan (BCP/DR) that covers restoration of ePHI systems, defines RTO and RPO for ePHI-processing systems, includes an emergency mode operation procedure for maintaining ePHI access during system failures, and has been tested and revised in the past 12 months?
evidence: Business continuity plan, disaster recovery plan, RTO/RPO documentation for ePHI systems, emergency mode operation procedures, contingency plan test records and revision log, list of critical ePHI applications with continuity priorities
notes: HIPAA §164.308(a)(7) Contingency Plan — covers §164.308(a)(7)(ii) Disaster Recovery Plan (R), §164.308(a)(7)(iii) Emergency Mode Operation Plan (R), §164.308(a)(7)(iv) Testing and Revision (A); also supports §164.310(a)(1)(i) Contingency Operations — physical access to ePHI during emergencies (A)

csf_subcategory_name: Organizational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk
question: Do you have documented procedures for verifying that workforce members are authorized before granting access to ePHI, and for promptly revoking all ePHI access upon termination, role change, or disciplinary action?
evidence: Access authorization procedure or pre-access approval workflow, offboarding checklist with ePHI access revocation steps, HR-to-IT termination workflow documentation, evidence of timely access removal (sample termination tickets or access removal records), workforce clearance verification records if applicable
notes: HIPAA §164.308(a)(3) Workforce Security — §164.308(a)(3)(i) Authorization and/or Supervision (A), §164.308(a)(3)(ii) Workforce Clearance Procedure (A), §164.308(a)(3)(iii) Termination Procedures (A)

csf_subcategory_name: Identities and credentials for authorized users and services are managed
question: Do you apply role-based access controls to limit each workforce member's access to ePHI to the minimum necessary for their job function, and are access rights reviewed periodically to confirm they remain appropriate?
evidence: RBAC configuration documentation, access control matrix mapping roles to ePHI data scope, role definitions with data access boundaries, periodic access review records (at least annually or upon role change)
notes: HIPAA §164.308(a)(4) Information Access Management — minimum necessary principle limits ePHI access to the minimum required for job function; complements §164.308(a)(4)(ii) Access Authorization and §164.308(a)(4)(iii) Access Establishment and Modification assessed in governance checklist via PR.AA-01 and PR.AA-03
